project_intent.goal: Make services first-class sellable and bookable entities inside offers, replacing the linked_product workaround with a service-native model.
instructions_for_claude: Treat this as a full implementation spec, not a brainstorm.
Use AWS-only infrastructure patterns and Python backend code.
Use DynamoDB for all persistent storage.
Implement only backend and data model changes in this phase.
Retire linked_product entirely: it already exists in this codebase (schema field), so remove its behavior rather than migrate it.
Preserve current behaviors unless this spec explicitly changes them.
Preserve the existing direct booking flow (/book/{service} reserve -> checkout -> pay) as a first-class entry point; migrate it to the shared pricing model.
Optimize for minimal ambiguity and implementation readiness.

source_of_truth: Service is the source of truth for scheduling and service pricing.
Offer is the source of truth for what the customer sees and buys.
Appointment is the source of truth for booking state.
Invoice is the source of truth for book-then-pay payment collection.
design_constraints: Support mixed offers containing products and services.
Do not create service-specific pricing logic outside the shared pricing model.
Keep booking and invoicing decoupled but linkable through IDs and metadata.

epics.name: Service Data Model
epics.objective: Refactor service storage to use shared pricing objects and support first-class offer inclusion.

stories.name: Migrate service pricing to prices[]
stories.description: Represent service pricing using the shared Price primitive with default_price_id as the selector.
acceptance_criteria: Service stores prices[] instead of only a single price.
default_price_id points to a valid price in prices[].

Existing services with a legacy single price are migrated: on read, a lazy adapter synthesizes prices[] and default_price_id from the legacy price so all consumers see the new shape even for un-migrated documents; the migrated shape is written forward on the next save.
Legacy price may be read only through that back-compat adapter.
Service pricing supports standard, sale, and flash_sale contexts (upsell/order_bump/downsell are funnel-only and excluded for services).
Service pricing supports standard and net_guaranteed fee handling.
pricing_model is one_time for services in this phase; recurring and customer_chooses are out of scope.
implementation_notes: Keep price schema identical to the shared primitive.
Do not add service-only pricing fields that duplicate shared Price fields.
Provide the read adapter so appointment/checkout/invoice code paths never branch on legacy vs migrated service documents.

stories.name: Add booking_flow to service
stories.description: Store a service default booking flow to drive checkout and scheduling behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Each service has a booking_flow value.
Allowed values are book_then_pay and pay_then_book.
The backend can override service default booking_flow at the offer item level.
implementation_notes: Use this as the default behavior only; offer items may override it.

epics.name: Offer Item Expansion
epics.objective: Allow offers to include services as first-class sellable items.

stories.name: Support service_id offer items
stories.description: Extend offer item schema so an item can reference either a product or a service.
acceptance_criteria: Offer items support product_id or service_id.
Exactly one of product_id or service_id is required per item.
Service items reference a service price_id from Service.prices[] via the fixed price_id path only; selectable_prices (packages) for services are out of scope for this phase.
Service items must share the offer's product_intent=transaction and stripe_mode, consistent with existing offer invariants.
Offer resolution returns a uniform resolved shape for both products and services.
implementation_notes: Retire linked_product behavior entirely.
Maintain compatibility with existing product offer behavior.
Extend the existing oneOf on offer items rather than replacing it.

stories.name: Support per-item booking flow override
stories.description: Allow each service item in an offer to override the service default booking flow.
acceptance_criteria: Offer service items may specify booking_flow.
Allowed values are book_then_pay and pay_then_book.
If omitted, service default booking_flow is used.
implementation_notes: This enables the same service to be sold differently on different offers.

epics.name: Appointment Linkage
epics.objective: Attach booking, offer, invoice, and page attribution to appointments.

stories.name: Add appointment source metadata
stories.description: Persist source attribution and related object IDs on appointments.
acceptance_criteria: Appointment stores order_id.
Appointment stores offer_id.
Appointment stores invoice_id.
Appointment stores page_id.
Appointment stores source with allowed values booking_page, offer, and invoice.
implementation_notes: Use these fields for reconciliation, analytics, and workflow routing.

stories.name: Add awaiting_schedule flag
stories.description: Represent a paid but unscheduled appointment cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: Appointment can exist with awaiting_schedule=true.
When awaiting_schedule=true, starts_at, ends_at, and timezone are optional/nullable and are populated at scheduling time.
Appointment transitions out of awaiting_schedule after scheduling.
Booked appointment state remains supported.
implementation_notes: Prefer a flag over introducing a new paid-but-unscheduled status.
Relax the Appointment schema and validate_appointment required fields so a paid-but-unscheduled appointment validates without a time; re-require them once scheduled.

epics.name: Pricing and Fees
epics.objective: Ensure services use the shared pricing and fee logic correctly.

stories.name: Resolve service pricing through shared pricing engine
stories.description: Use the existing pricing engine for services without bespoke logic.
acceptance_criteria: Service pricing resolves through the shared Price primitive.
Service items route to a defined platform fee rate; they must not silently fall through to the digital class by accident.
Fee handling supports standard and net_guaranteed.
compare_at and context values are honored.
implementation_notes: Known behavior: fee_class_for() currently returns 'digital' for any non-physical/non-tip_jar product_type, and the default billing config has no 'service' tier. So passing product_type='service' alone yields the digital rate.

Choose one and document it: (a) explicitly reuse the digital fee class for services (drop the 'service class' language), or (b) add a 'service' branch to fee_class_for AND 'service' rates under platform_fees.tiers for every plan.
Do not fork the pricing engine.

epics.name: Booking and Invoicing Workflows
epics.objective: Support pay-then-book, book-then-pay, and standalone invoicing through backend logic.

stories.name: Implement pay-then-book workflow
stories.description: Allow a customer to pay first and schedule afterward.
acceptance_criteria: Checkout completion creates an order and an appointment.
The appointment is marked payment_status=paid.
The appointment is marked awaiting_schedule=true.
Appointment creation is idempotent on the checkout session / payment_intent (deterministic appointment_id or existence check), so a duplicate or redelivered session cannot create two appointments.
Customer can later schedule the appointment using the manage-token protected flow.
implementation_notes: Make the scheduling step idempotent.
Do not require invoice creation in this flow.

stories.name: Implement book-then-pay workflow
stories.description: Allow a customer to book first and pay later through invoicing.
acceptance_criteria: Appointment can be created with status booked and payment_status=unpaid.
Invoice can be created from the appointment.
Invoice source links back to appointment_id.
invoice.paid updates appointment payment_status to paid.

The unpaid-booked lifecycle is defined: state whether/when an unpaid booked appointment expires or auto-cancels and whether it holds the slot until paid. Default: it holds the slot and does not auto-expire; a tenant policy may override.
implementation_notes: Invoice sending must be configurable.
Default behavior may be one-click send, but not hard-coded.

stories.name: Preserve standalone invoicing
stories.description: Keep invoice creation and payment available without offers or landing pages.
acceptance_criteria: Tenant can create a standalone invoice without an offer.
Tenant can create a standalone invoice without a page.
Standalone invoicing still supports service lines.
implementation_notes: Treat this as a first-class supported path, not a fallback.

stories.name: Define mixed product + service offer behavior
stories.description: Support (or explicitly restrict) offers that contain both products and services in one checkout.
acceptance_criteria: A single checkout may contain product lines and service lines.
Each service line produces its own appointment following that item's resolved booking_flow.
Product lines are fulfilled through existing product fulfillment with no scheduling.
One payment covers the whole cart; scheduling for pay_then_book service lines happens after payment.
If full mixed-cart support is deferred, mixed offers are rejected at validation time rather than silently mishandled.
implementation_notes: Reuse the existing single-offer checkout; do not introduce a new cart primitive in this phase.

stories.name: Preserve the direct booking (schedule-then-pay) flow
stories.description: Keep the existing /book/{service} reserve -> checkout -> pay flow working and migrate it to shared pricing.
acceptance_criteria: The existing schedule-then-pay booking flow continues to function unchanged in behavior.
It resolves price via prices[]/default_price_id (through the legacy read adapter) instead of the single legacy price.
It honors fee_handling (standard and net_guaranteed) from the resolved price, delivering net-guaranteed on the direct booking path.
implementation_notes: This is the third entry point alongside offer-driven and standalone-invoice; do not remove it.

epics.name: Backend API and Event Handling
epics.objective: Expose the endpoints and event handlers needed to support the new service flows.

stories.name: Update checkout session creation
stories.description: Create checkout sessions from service offer items and include booking metadata.
acceptance_criteria: Checkout builder resolves service items correctly.
Checkout metadata includes service_id, booking_flow, offer_id, and page_id.
Service purchase flow follows the selected booking timing.
implementation_notes: Do not allow service-offer checkout to bypass required metadata.

stories.name: Update webhook handling
stories.description: Process paid sessions and invoice events for service flows safely.
acceptance_criteria: checkout.completed creates order and appointment for pay-then-book.
invoice.paid updates appointment payment status for book-then-pay.
Webhook processing is idempotent.
Duplicate webhook events do not create duplicate side effects.
implementation_notes: Use existing webhook event tracking or equivalent idempotency storage.

stories.name: Update booking endpoints
acceptance_criteria: Booking endpoints can schedule an awaiting_schedule appointment.
Scheduling re-validates live availability and claims the slot via the existing slot-lock mechanism before committing, preventing double-booking of a taken slot.
Scheduling clears awaiting_schedule.
Scheduling sets starts_at and ends_at.
For pay_then_book appointments, the compensation snapshot (rule_snapshot) is taken at scheduling time from the chosen fulfiller, not at purchase time.
implementation_notes: Require manage-token authorization.
Reuse the existing available_slots + SlotLockRepository conditional-claim path used by reserve; do not add a parallel availability check.

stories.name: Update invoice endpoints
acceptance_criteria: Invoice can be generated from an appointment.
Invoice retains source relationship to appointment.
Invoice can be sent according to configuration.
implementation_notes: Support explicit send and auto-send modes if already part of platform behavior.

epics.name: Security and Integrity
epics.objective: Ensure the new service model remains isolated, safe, and tenant-correct.

stories.name: Enforce environment isolation
stories.description: Keep test and live data fully separated.
acceptance_criteria: Offers, services, appointments, invoices, and checkout sessions remain partitioned by mode.
Test-mode records cannot leak into live mode or vice versa.

stories.name: Enforce authorization and token gating
stories.description: Protect scheduling and other sensitive mutations.
acceptance_criteria: Scheduling purchased appointments requires valid manage-token authorization.
Tenant-aware access controls apply to all backend mutations.
IAM permissions are least privilege.

system_level: A service can be sold through an offer as a first-class item.
A service can be booked and paid through both book-then-pay and pay-then-book flows.
The direct booking (schedule-then-pay) flow still works and uses the shared pricing model, including net_guaranteed.
Standalone invoicing still works without offers or landing pages.
Pricing uses the shared Price primitive everywhere.
Legacy single-price services resolve through the prices[] read adapter without breaking any consumer.
linked_product is no longer part of the implementation.
The backend remains serverless on AWS with DynamoDB as the database.
Webhook and booking operations are idempotent and secure.

high_level_success_tests: Offer resolution returns the correct service price and service name.
Mixed product and service offers resolve correctly.
Service pricing applies fee logic correctly in both standard and net_guaranteed modes.
pay_then_book creates a paid appointment with awaiting_schedule=true and allows later scheduling.
Scheduling a purchased appointment re-checks availability and cannot double-book a slot already taken by another appointment.
book_then_pay creates an unpaid booked appointment and marks it paid after invoice payment.
The direct schedule-then-pay booking flow works and applies net_guaranteed pricing from prices[].
Legacy single-price services resolve correctly via the prices[] read adapter.
Mixed product+service offers either resolve and check out correctly or are explicitly rejected at validation.
Standalone invoice creation works without an offer or page.
Duplicate webhook events do not create duplicate records or state transitions.

security: Apply tenant-aware authorization on all backend mutations.
Use least-privilege IAM for AWS resources.
Gate purchase scheduling with manage tokens.
Separate test and live data absolutely.
reliability: All payment-triggered and booking-triggered handlers must be idempotent.
Do not rely on UI behavior for correctness.
maintainability: Keep pricing, booking, and invoicing logic modular.
Prefer shared primitives and shared handlers over duplicated service-specific logic.

phase_1_backend_only: Service schema refactor (prices[] + legacy-price read adapter/migration)
Pricing and fee logic updates (service fee-class decision)
Direct booking flow migration to shared pricing
Checkout and webhook backend updates (idempotent appointment creation)
Booking and invoice endpoint updates (schedule with availability re-check + slot lock)
phase_2_ui: Customer-facing booking and invoice UX updates
